Welcome to our committee. Anytime you're ready. Thank you Madam Chair. Thank you members of the committee. This is House Memorial 65. And I'm just going to pull it up on my screen here. On House Memorial 65 is requesting that the Department of Health work together with the Department of Public Safety to convene a task force and conduct some research on health and firearm safety policies and make recommendations on and suggestions on how to reduce firearm violence in New Mexico. This memorial came about after many meetings with community and those meetings began because of concerns that were raised by my constituents in during my campaign. There were families who, or whatever reason, have been more affected by gun violence and families who were afraid to send their children to school because they were afraid of massive shootings and families who just brought up that they, maybe they had guns and they wanted to protect their rights to have guns in the district. And so rather than looking at the issue of firearm safety as gun control or gun rights, the suggestion from the group that worked on this memorial was to look at it as a health issue and to look at it as gun safety and the reason it came up as a health issue is because one of the members that is in our group, one of our leaders is a doctor. He's a geriatric specialist. He attends the Medical Association conferences every year and the number one issue that came up as the health issue was gun violence, gun deaths, gun deaths. When we look at this as a health issue, we can look at it either as a medical issue or we can look at as a public health issue. Medical issue meaning you're shot, you go to the emergency room, you, may not live, but the doctors try to treat you. Public health meaning we have an issue, let's study it, let's look at what is causing the deaths because Madam Chair, in the research that we did in this short period of time, we have approximately 330,000 guns in our country but not that this is a small number, 40,000 deaths. So how is it that 290,000 guns are kept safe, used safely but some guns are used in other ways that was harm. We believe that this is an issue that we can start to look at through the department of health and public safety, look for some patterns, come up with some ideas for what might be causing and what might be some creative solutions, look to see how those ideas work and then continue conversations around here's what we found, here's what we saw, here's what we tried and here's what's working in our community because it's not just cities, it's also rural communities, guns are everywhere in the state and people use them everywhere in the state, disproportionately gun violence affects males of color. However, two thirds of the deaths are white males. So there's a lot of data that's really interesting that we believe we need to look at as a health issue similar to how we looked at car safety in the 1960s and 70s when our deaths were increasing with car safety. We didn't stop the production of cars, in fact we have more cars now than we did in the 1960s but we looked at tools and ideas for how to lessen injury and lessen deaths, things like the seat belts that we use now, the air cushions that we use now in cars, things like car seats have saved thousands of lives. So what can we do to save lives with firearm safety? So Madam Chair, we bring this to you from people who have worked in my area, which is House District 30 and also from people who have worked in neighboring communities. We also went to different organizations where one of our leaders was asked to speak about some data related to history, related to guns. And so we reached out to different parts of Albuquerque and there's strong interest in collecting some data doing some research, Madam Chair. You, let's go to the audience. Do we have any supporting comments opposing webcasts? Kathleen Mosley? Can you hear me? Yes. Dear, dear Johnson and members of the committee, thanks for bringing and sponsor represented Velasquez. My professor in my public health program was murdered in one of our country's earliest mass school shooting incidents, left six dead and one paralyzed. And according to the K through 12 school shooting database, there have now been almost 3,000 school shooting incidents in the US, which is a staggering number, not seen among our peers in Europe. And in New Mexico, we're not immune, experiencing over two dozen incidents in that same timeframe. Public health wise, 4% of all New Mexico high school children interviewed through the, you risk behavior survey, say they brought a weapon to school in the last 30 days. And one high school student, as we know, this last August at West Mesa, brought a high capacity magazine that was loaded with 21 cartridges to school. House Memorial 65 places firearm safety, comprehensive and systematic approaches in the purview of the health department with the public safety department, providing them to make plans with the public health approach. Using, and that means using data, as Representative Velasquez mentioned, policy and regulation, community based programs, policy at points of sale, manufacturing and design, distribution and accessibility of firearms, and ammunition, technology, and storage practices. Takes a lot of courage to act, to do something that evidence shows will make a difference. And to initiate action that keeps people safe, given that there are guns in the environment. You, Madam Chair and thank you, Representative and I appreciate that and I do wanna, maybe I don't know where it could fall into this because it talks about community and prevention stakeholders that may vaguely be who that might be but I would like to see that from that perspective. And then another question here on page four we're talking about folks on the, on the committee, the task force and we talk about police chiefs of Albuquerque Las Cruces or Santa Fe. Absolutely. And so your question, I'm not sure if I answered that in terms of how do we address it but it was intended to capture a couple of issues that are starting to come up with looking at the data. Thank you Madam Chair and Representative and I appreciate that and I think that when it comes to, I haven't seen the data on that so I would have to look at that before voting on this but I know in places like Washington state and places that have a lot of rain even across the pond over in Europe like England there are high suicide rates because there's a lack of sunlight and stuff. So I've heard like the argument now that with global warming or with changes in climate that is happening and so I'm wondering in New Mexico we have so much sunlight and you just help me with that. I mean, we've always been sunny. I mean, I don't know if it's getting hotter but maybe if you have some information on me. Madam Chair, Representative, you are absolutely right in areas where we have lessened sunlight, lessened opportunity for vitamin D, lessened opportunity for that sunlight to hit our eyes which automatically does something to our physical body that energizes and brings us into a better mood that yes, that does affect gun violence as well. So you're absolutely right. We want the opportunity to really look at everything that affects New Mexico in particular but recognizing what happens in other states is also very important, Madam Chair, in terms of looking at use of funds. Madam Chair, Representative, and I haven't looked at all that data and so I don't feel really comfortable voting for this right now because I haven't looked at that but I do want to just say, I appreciate you for taking this on. I do, I want to end violence period in our communities and especially when it comes to suicides in my district we have a lot of veterans and I hate to hear when there's a veteran suicide or any suicide. When it comes to things like I had a cousin she killed herself unfortunately and she didn't do it through a gun and I want to definitely talk a lot about how we can prevent that and just any kind of violence in our communities whether it be throughout related or gang related or any other kind of related. So I appreciate the conversation today and I thank you and I also love guns by the way. I love guns so much. Everyone already knows that but I want to make sure that we have as many people owning firearms and then safely owning them and doing those courses making sure that they have their skills ready being a really good shot. So if someone breaks into your house, you don't miss so Madam Chair representative thank you for the conversation today. So that's the reason that I can't support it at this time because of the data I haven't seen and I would love my more tight language when it comes to who's gonna be on the task force especially from rural areas and then those stakeholders like the gun safety course folks but I will definitely continue to listen to the conversation and thank you so much to Representative I don't know if you have anything to add to that. Yes Madam Chair one of the assumptions that we make is that gun deaths are correlated with mental health and that's one of the issues that we want to look at and that relates to the question we had around suicide I believe and so looking at looking at the data would be very helpful in helping us to address not just gun violence but also those suicides that are happening that may or may not be gun related. So being my first term still I'm still learning and I did not provide a lot of data but there's some data in your memorial there that can be very interesting for you I'm happy to provide websites that provide more data but in terms of the state it's not something that we're collecting yet in being able or we're not using the process yet in terms of being able to really look at the patterns of what's happening and that's what we really want to do is to start looking at those patterns so that we can identify some solutions. Thank you Madam Chair, thank you Representative and I think that's a big topic. And I would be happy to change it for next time. Oh no you're fine, thank you Madam Chair as representative so I would suggest just maybe even bring this up to the Chair of the Health Committee in the interim and if we can have a conversation about suicides preventing that kind of that topic and then just violence in general and maybe something on a public health perspective on how we could do that. So regardless of if your memorial passes or for the stars or whatever, I mean I wish you the best and I think it's a good idea to talk about these things because they affect all of our communities. So thank you Madam Chair and thank you Representative. Madam Chair. Thank you, Representative Buhon. So the purpose of the task force would be to really work together to get more information and data and specifically to New Mexico as we already do know that we're in the highest states with what I took from this for suicide and got gun-related suicides in our states. I think that this is, I think you're hitting on the point and keeping this close to where half the human services and health is in accordance with this. So the task, you list the composition of the task force, is that correct? Yes, Madam Chair. We made suggestions for who should serve on the task force looking first at entities that would be important to involve in terms of not only collecting the data but also making sense of it because you can have some data but unless you really know the different aspects of how that data touches your lives, you might miss something. So that's why numerous entities were included in those categories. Thank you. Thank you. I do like this. I think we've been talking about this a bit. I think that in the second district, the judicial district, Madam Chair, there is a task force related, I think of it off the top of my head but we just gave some funding to it through the budget. So I would say that there is a tension and I would look to this, like in district, is that it's held in the DA's office. So I just make that extra recommendation, show that you are on the same track of what is going on, particularly with us giving extra funding for that Madam Chair. Thank you, I support you, Madam Chair. Madam Chair. Thank you. Madam Vice Chair and then Representative Onionargo. Thank you, Madam Chair. Madam Chair, Representative, thank you so much for bringing this forward. I think it's going to be in the efforts that I've been working on in the last several years. I think this will win some opportunities for more information. Our standing is also very highly negative. That was a little weird. I couldn't find the words this morning but I appreciate that. The other important piece as it has already been mentioned here, Madam Chair and Representative, is the suicide rate. Unfortunately, we've known in a rural community is what's happening and the loss that comes not just to the family but an entire community is huge. Unfortunately, that intersection of, as it states in the resolution and the memorial, the intersection of these two, I think, is something that's really going to, that could shed some important light on developing policies for the future. And finally, and of my statement, it's being a family that was affected by gun violence. It's really important to me. And I do not want any family to have to go through this was mentioned by my colleagues. It's something you never, ever want to have happen and to the extent that we can prevent some of these and find the reasons is truly important. Thank you, Madam Chair. My question then, Madam Representative, is I'm happy to see that there's an end date for the report to be. You're going to be studying this this summer and I saw the end date. I couldn't see it at first and that was my question. But I see the end date is September 15th. Can you please tell me what your timeline is looking at? What you're looking at for this group or what you're expected meeting times. Maybe perhaps how you will be assembled. And one final note is that I see in our analysis report there's actually a list of possible entities that might be part of this task force, which I really appreciate it because I was trying to think who else might be and maybe make some suggestions. So if you could answer that, provide some information on the timeline, I would appreciate it. Thank you, but thank you, Madam Chair. Yes, thank you, Madam Chair, Madam Vice Chair. And just in case you hadn't noticed, I'm joined by Senator Pinto. And Senator Pinto had proposed a memorial either last year or the year before and is proposing a similar memorial this year to the one that we are proposing here. And so I'm sure the feedback and the questions you all are providing will be very helpful in her memorial as well. In terms of the timelines, what we had thought was monthly meetings and I can't believe I left that out. We did a lot of editing somehow that got taken out but monthly meetings and the September 15th deadline was so that the report could be given to the health committee and the interim. And because we can give updates, but until we have a good solid report, we really can't start making suggestions in the health committee. And so we want to be able to make some suggestions in terms of what the health committee is seeing in the interim and work from there. Madam Chair, was there anything Senator that you would like to add? Thank you, Madam Chair, that was all my questions. Thank you. Representative Anya Nournou. I'm trying to move a due pass. On House Memorial 65 is requesting
that the Department of Health work together
with the Department of Public Safety
to convene a task force and conduct some research
on health and firearm safety policies
and make recommendations on and suggestions
on how to reduce firearm violence in New Mexico.
This memorial came about after many meetings with community
and those meetings began because of concerns
that were raised by my constituents
in during my campaign.
There were families who, or whatever reason,
have been more affected by gun violence
and families who were afraid to send their children to school
because they were afraid of massive shootings
and families who just brought up that they,
maybe they had guns and they wanted to protect their rights
to have guns in the district.
And so rather than looking at the issue of firearm safety
as gun control or gun rights,
the suggestion from the group that worked on this memorial
was to look at it as a health issue
and to look at it as gun safety and the reason
it came up as a health issue is because one of the members
that is in our group, one of our leaders is a doctor.
He's a geriatric specialist.
He attends the Medical Association conferences every year
and the number one issue that came up as the health issue
was gun violence, gun deaths, gun deaths.
When we look at this as a health issue,
we can look at it either as a medical issue
or we can look at as a public health issue.
Medical issue meaning you're shot,
you go to the emergency room, you,
may not live, but the doctors try to treat you.
Public health meaning we have an issue, let's study it,
let's look at what is causing the deaths
because Madam Chair, in the research that we did
in this short period of time,
we have approximately 330,000 guns in our country
but not that this is a small number, 40,000 deaths.
So how is it that 290,000 guns are kept safe, used safely
but some guns are used in other ways that was harm.
We believe that this is an issue
that we can start to look at through the department
of health and public safety, look for some patterns,
come up with some ideas for what might be causing
and what might be some creative solutions,
look to see how those ideas work
and then continue conversations around here's what we found,
here's what we saw, here's what we tried
and here's what's working in our community
because it's not just cities, it's also rural communities,
guns are everywhere in the state
and people use them everywhere in the state,
disproportionately gun violence affects males of color.
However, two thirds of the deaths are white males.
So there's a lot of data that's really interesting
that we believe we need to look at as a health issue
similar to how we looked at car safety in the 1960s and 70s
when our deaths were increasing with car safety.
We didn't stop the production of cars,
in fact we have more cars now than we did in the 1960s
but we looked at tools and ideas for how to lessen injury

that we use now, the air cushions
that we use now in cars, things like car seats
have saved thousands of lives.
